questionText: Let $x$ be a real number. Determine whether the condition $x > 5$ is (a) a sufficient condition, and (b) a necessary condition, for $x > 2$. Justify each answer carefully.

steps.description: Set up the two conditions as P and Q.
steps.explanation: We label the conditions so we can test the two directions of implication. 'Sufficient' and 'necessary' are just names for those two directions.

steps.description: State what sufficient and necessary mean here.
steps.workingLatex: P \text{ sufficient for } Q \Leftrightarrow (P \Rightarrow Q); \quad P \text{ necessary for } Q \Leftrightarrow (Q \Rightarrow P)
steps.explanation: $P$ is sufficient for $Q$ if $P$ alone guarantees $Q$; $P$ is necessary for $Q$ if $Q$ cannot happen without $P$. These are opposite directions, so we check each in turn.

steps.description: Picture the two conditions as sets on the number line.
steps.explanation: Every number bigger than $5$ is automatically bigger than $2$, so the set for $P$ sits entirely inside the set for $Q$. This picture predicts both results before any algebra.

steps.description: Test sufficiency: assume P and deduce Q.
steps.explanation: If $x$ exceeds $5$ then it certainly exceeds $2$, since $5$ is already greater than $2$. So $P$ forces $Q$ and the forward implication holds.

steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \text{ true, so } P \text{ is sufficient}
steps.explanation: Because assuming $P$ always delivers $Q$, the condition $x>5$ is enough on its own to secure $x>2$.
For $P$ to be necessary, $Q$ would have to fail whenever $P$ fails. The value $x=3$ satisfies $Q$ without satisfying $P$, so $Q$ can hold without $P$.

steps.workingLatex: Q \not\Rightarrow P, \text{ so } P \text{ is not necessary}
steps.explanation: Since $x=3$ meets $x>2$ while missing $x>5$, the condition $x>5$ is not required for $x>2$. Numbers between $2$ and $5$ satisfy $Q$ on their own.

steps.workingLatex: P \text{ sufficient but not necessary for } Q
steps.explanation: The forward test passed and the reverse test failed, so $x>5$ is sufficient but not necessary for $x>2$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $x > 5$ is a sufficient condition for $x > 2$ (since $x>5 \Rightarrow x>2$) but not a necessary one (e.g. $x=3$ gives $x>2$ without $x>5$).
